scpub1.translation_subtitle: An approachable translation of the Theragāthā
scpub1.translation_process: Translated from the Pali. The primary source was the Mahāsaṅgīti edition, with reference to several English translations, especially those of K.R. Norman.
scpub1.translation_description: This translation aims to make a clear, readable, and accurate rendering of the Theragāthā. The initial edition was by Jessica Walton and Bhikkhu Sujato was published in 2014 through SuttaCentral. A revised edition, bringing the terminology in line with the subsequently-translated four Nikāyas, was published in 2019.

scpub4.translation_subtitle: A plain translation of the Saṁyutta Nikāya
scpub4.translation_process: Primary source was the digital Mahāsaṅgīti edition of the Pali Tipiṭaka. Translated from the Pali, with reference to several English translations, especially those of Bhikkhu Bodhi.
scpub4.translation_description: This translation was part of a project to translate the four Pali Nikāyas with the following aims: plain, approachable English; consistent terminology; accurate rendition of the Pali; free of copyright. It was made during 2016–2018 while Bhikkhu Sujato was staying in Qimei, Taiwan.

scpub7.translation_subtitle: A meaningful translation of the Dhammapada
scpub7.translation_process: Translated from the Pali. The primary source was the Mahāsaṅgīti edition, with reference to several English translations, especially those of K.R. Norman and Venerable Buddharakkhita.
scpub7.translation_description: This translation aims to make a clear, readable, and accurate rendering of the Dhammapada. Unlike most Dhammapadas in English, this is a new translation from the source Pali text. The aim was to make the sense as transparent as possible.

scpub20.translation_subtitle: A Translation of the Chinese Saṃyukta Āgama
scpub20.translation_process: The Related Discourses is translated directly from classical Chinese using Yinshun’s [雜阿含經論會編](https://cbetaonline.cn/zh/Y0030_001) as the root text while consulting the Taisho Daizokyo (T99) and CBETA editions and parallels found in Chinese, Pali, and Sanskrit. English translations consulted include translations of SĀ sutras by Bhikkhu Anālayo as well as English translations of the Pali Nikāyas by Bhikkhu Sujato and Bhikkhu Bodhi.
scpub20.translation_description: This translation is part of Charles Patton’s [Dharma Pearls Project](https://dharmapearls.net/) to translate Chinese EBTs into modern English and provide them free to the public. This project began in 2019 and is an ongoing work-in-progress.
scpub20.publication_status: This is an ongoing translation project in its early stage. Translations of individual sutras are initially released at Dharma Pearls and mirrored at SuttaCentral.

scpub39.translation_subtitle: Texts from the Taisho Tripitaka edited for SuttaCentral
scpub39.translation_process: The [SAT 2018](https://21dzk.l.u-tokyo.ac.jp/SAT2018/) source texts have been corrected and re-punctuated. Most CBETA corrections have been adopted and documented as well as occasional textual corruptions. Variant readings include those from the Taisho, CBETA, and Yinshun’s edition of the Saṁyuktāgama (T99). The text is then reviewed twice and segmented to match English translations by Charles Patton.
scpub39.translation_description: A critical edition of the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō (大正新脩大藏經) with improved punctuation and corrections.
scpub39.publication_status: This is an ongoing project. Texts are prepared individually and added to SuttaCentral as they are completed.

scpub53.translation_process: Translated from the Pāli. Primary source was the Burmese Sixth Council edition. In the interest of being as true as possible to the original Pāli text, this translation has been prepared without influence from the so-called “Dhammapada Commentary” (an apocryphal work that deviates significantly from the root text).
scpub53.translation_description: A new translation of the Dhammapada, with a particular focus on accuracy and fidelity to the root text.

scpub64.translation_title: The Buddhist Era 2500 Great International Council Pāḷi Tipiṭaka
scpub64.translation_subtitle: 40 Volume Edition in Roman Script B.E. 2548 (2005)
scpub64.translation_process: Prepared by the M.L. Maniratana Bunnag Dhamma Society Fund based on the Vipassanā Research Institute Tipiṭaka, 2537–2542, which was originally known as Chaṭṭha Saṅgāyana CD-ROM and is available at tipitaka.org. The entire text was proofread, and in addition, recited four times between B.E. 2543–2545 (2000–2002) and B.E. 2549–2550 (2006–2007) to verify every Pāḷi sound and to correct the printing errors of the original 40-volume edition. The primary reference for proofreading was the first edition of the Chaṭṭha Saṅgāyana Tipiṭaka published in Myanmar in 1958. Additional reference was made to 18 editions of the Pāḷi Tipiṭaka in various national scripts of other countries.
scpub64.translation_description: 40 volumes of the Pali Tipiṭaka in Roman script. Includes the Nettiparaṇa, Peṭakopadesa, and Milindapañha. The Pali text on SuttaCentral was extracted from the source XML files of the Mahāsaṅgīti edition. Minor editorial changes have been made to numbering and punctuation, but no changes to the text itself.

license.license_statement: This work is an ancient sacred text that was created thousands of years ago and is maintained to the present day by its traditional custodians, the Buddhist monastic Sangha. It is free of known restrictions under copyright law, including all related and neighboring rights. We respectfully request that it is used in accordance with the values of the Buddhist tradition.

scpub69.translation_subtitle: A translation of the Patna Dhamma Verses
scpub69.translation_description: The Patna Dharmapada was transcribed by Dr Margaret Cone and published in the Journal of the Pali Text Society, Volume XIII (1989), pp. 101-217. Dr Cone's text was reproduced with the consent of the Pali Text Society, Oxford, England. Pāḷi Parallels, Metrical Commentary, Notes and Indexes were compiled and translated by Ānandajoti Bhikkhu (2017).
